report_name: Vimeo Data Exposed in Supply-Chain Attack on Vendor Anodot; ShinyHunters Implicated

threat_actors.name: ShinyHunters
threat_actors.description: A notorious extortion group implicated in a supply-chain attack against Anodot, which resulted in the compromise of data from Vimeo and Rockstar Games. The group listed Vimeo on its data leak site.
threat_actors.goals: Data theft for the purpose of extortion.

attack_patterns.name: Steal Application Access Token
attack_patterns.description: Attackers stole authentication tokens from the third-party analytics vendor, Anodot, to gain unauthorized access to its customers' cloud environments like Snowflake and BigQuery.

attack_patterns.name: Data from Cloud Storage
attack_patterns.description: The attackers used stolen authentication tokens to directly access and exfiltrate data, including video metadata and customer emails, from Vimeo's Snowflake and BigQuery cloud data warehouses.

attack_patterns.name: Trusted Relationship
attack_patterns.description: The attackers exploited the trusted relationship between Vimeo and its analytics vendor, Anodot. By compromising the vendor, they bypassed Vimeo's direct perimeter defenses to access their data.

identities.name: Vimeo
identities.description: A video hosting platform whose user and customer data was exposed as a result of a supply-chain attack on its third-party analytics vendor, Anodot.

identities.name: Anodot
identities.description: A third-party analytics vendor for Vimeo. Anodot was compromised, and authentication tokens were stolen from its systems, leading to a supply-chain attack against its customers.

identities.name: Rockstar Games
identities.description: A video game company identified as another victim of the same Anodot compromise perpetrated by ShinyHunters.

infrastructure.name: ShinyHunters Data Leak Site
infrastructure.description: The data leak site where the ShinyHunters group claimed responsibility for the attack and listed data stolen from Vimeo.

indicators.name: Anomalous User-Agent for Third-Party Vendor Access
indicators.pattern:
title: Anomalous User-Agent for Third-Party Vendor Access
id: 9c5b1a3d-8e4f-4b1a-9c8d-6f5e4d3c2b1a
status: experimental
description: >
  Detects API or web access from a known third-party vendor service account that uses an unusual or non-standard User-Agent string.
  This could indicate the use of stolen credentials or tokens with a custom script rather than the legitimate vendor application, as seen in the Anodot breach affecting Vimeo.
references:
  - https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/security/video-service-vimeo-confirms-anodot-breach-exposed-user-data/
author: cyber.netsecops.io
date: 2026-05-04
tags:
  - attack.t1528
  - attack.credential_access
falsepositives:
  - Legitimate but undocumented tools used by the vendor.
  - Changes in the vendor's application stack.
level: medium
logsource:
  category: proxy
detection:
  selection:
    # This is a template. Replace with the specific vendor's service account name.
    c-user: 'anodot_service_account'
  filter:
    # Replace with the known-good User-Agent strings for the vendor.
    c-useragent:
      - 'AnodotAnalytics/1.0'
      - 'Go-http-client/1.1'
  condition: selection and not filter
indicators.description: Detects API or web access from a known third-party vendor service account that uses an unusual or non-standard User-Agent string, which could indicate the use of stolen tokens with a custom script rather than the legitimate vendor application.

relationships.source_name: Anomalous User-Agent for Third-Party Vendor Access
